title: What Is Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?
content:
When a cultural resource management (CRM) survey identifies a potential archaeological site, project planners face a critical question: is this site significant enough to warrant protection or avoidance? Phase II archaeological evaluation is the standardized process used to answer that question — and the results directly shape what happens next in any federally regulated project.

Quick Answer: Phase II archaeological evaluation is a targeted investigation that tests whether a site identified during a Phase I survey meets the criteria for listing on the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP). It involves systematic excavation, artifact analysis, and a formal eligibility determination required under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act.

Understanding Phase II evaluation is essential for federal agencies, state transportation departments, and private developers who must comply with Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA). Skipping or mishandling this step can halt a project, trigger regulatory penalties, or result in the inadvertent destruction of irreplaceable cultural resources.

This guide explains what Phase II archaeological evaluation involves, how it differs from Phase I survey, what criteria are used for significance determinations, and what the findings mean for your project timeline and budget.

How Does Phase II Evaluation Differ from Phase I Survey?

Phase I survey is a broad reconnaissance designed to locate and identify archaeological sites across a project area. Phase II evaluation is a focused, more intensive investigation of specific sites already found during Phase I to determine whether they meet NRHP eligibility standards.

Where Phase I typically involves pedestrian survey, shovel testing on a regular interval, and site boundary mapping, Phase II goes deeper — literally and analytically. Archaeologists design a systematic testing strategy tailored to each site's characteristics, research questions, and the types of data needed to evaluate its significance.

The primary goal of Phase II is not to fully excavate or document a site, but to gather enough information to make a defensible eligibility recommendation. That recommendation is submitted to the lead federal agency and the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) for a formal determination. The Phase II scope is more limited than full data recovery, but far more rigorous than the initial reconnaissance.

What Are the NRHP Criteria Used in Phase II Evaluation?

Phase II evaluation assesses sites against the four criteria established in 36 CFR Part 60.4, the federal regulations governing the National Register of Historic Places. A site is considered significant if it meets at least one of these criteria and retains integrity.

Criterion A applies to sites associated with events that have made a significant contribution to the broad patterns of American history. Criterion B covers sites associated with the lives of significant persons. Criterion C addresses sites that embody the distinctive characteristics of a type, period, or method of construction, or represent the work of a master. Criterion D — the most commonly applied in archaeological contexts — recognizes sites that have yielded, or may be likely to yield, information important in history or prehistory.

Integrity is equally important. A site may have historical associations but lack the physical qualities — location, design, setting, materials, workmanship, feeling, and association — needed to convey its significance. Phase II evaluation documents both the site's associative value and its physical integrity to support a complete eligibility determination.

What Methods Are Used During Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?

Phase II evaluation typically relies on systematic hand-excavation of test units, carefully placed to sample different areas of the site and address specific research questions. Unit placement follows a research design developed before fieldwork begins.

Test unit sizes commonly range from 1x1 meter to 1x2 meters, excavated in stratigraphic levels or arbitrary levels depending on site conditions. All matrix from designated units is typically screened through ¼-inch hardware cloth, and finer screens may be used for sites with small or fragile artifacts. Feature excavation is also common — hearths, pits, postholes, and other subsurface features provide critical data about site function, chronology, and integrity.

Laboratory analysis follows fieldwork. Artifacts are cleaned, cataloged, identified, and interpreted. Samples for radiocarbon dating, flotation, or other specialist analyses may be submitted. GIS and remote sensing tools can supplement field data by modeling site distribution patterns or identifying subsurface anomalies. The final Phase II report synthesizes all findings into a formal eligibility recommendation with supporting evidence.

Who Reviews Phase II Findings and Makes the Final Eligibility Determination?

The lead federal agency — the entity with jurisdiction over the undertaking — bears ultimate responsibility for making the eligibility determination under Section 106. However, this decision is made in consultation with the SHPO and, where applicable, Tribal Historic Preservation Officers (THPOs) and other consulting parties.

The Phase II report prepared by the CRM firm is submitted to these parties for review. The SHPO typically provides a formal concurrence or objection. If all parties agree, the determination stands. If there is disagreement, the matter can be referred to the Advisory Council on Historic Preservation (ACHP) for resolution.

A finding of NRHP eligibility does not automatically stop a project. It triggers the next step: a Phase III data recovery or mitigation plan, or an agreement document outlining how the site's values will be addressed. A finding of ineligibility, by contrast, typically allows the project to proceed without further archaeological work in that area. Historic preservation compliance depends on accurate eligibility determinations at this stage.

How Long Does Phase II Evaluation Take and What Does It Cost?

Phase II evaluation timelines and costs vary considerably depending on site size, complexity, depth of deposits, and regulatory requirements. A straightforward evaluation of a small prehistoric site might be completed in a few weeks from fieldwork to final report. A complex multicomponent site with deep stratified deposits can take several months.

Typical Phase II projects involve one to three weeks of fieldwork, followed by laboratory analysis and report preparation. Laboratory work often takes longer than fieldwork, particularly if specialist analyses such as radiocarbon dating, zooarchaeological study, or paleoethnobotany are required.

Costs reflect this complexity. Small evaluations may run from $15,000 to $40,000; larger, more complex projects can exceed $100,000. Early coordination with your CRM firm and regulatory agencies helps scope the work appropriately, avoiding costly surprises. Project managers who engage qualified archaeologists early in project planning consistently achieve better cost and schedule outcomes than those who treat Phase II as an afterthought.

Frequently Asked Questions

What triggers a Phase II archaeological evaluation?

A Phase II evaluation is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies an archaeological site within a project's area of potential effect (APE) that may be eligible for the National Register of Historic Places. The lead federal agency, in consultation with the SHPO, determines whether Phase II evaluation is necessary before making an eligibility decision.

Can a project proceed if a site is found eligible during Phase II?

Yes. NRHP eligibility does not halt a project outright. If an eligible site will be adversely affected, the project must complete Section 106 consultation to determine appropriate mitigation — typically Phase III data recovery, avoidance, or a memorandum of agreement outlining protective measures before construction proceeds.

Who must conduct Phase II archaeological evaluation?

Phase II evaluation must be conducted by or under the direct supervision of a professional archaeologist who meets the Secretary of the Interior's Professional Qualification Standards — typically a master's degree and field experience in archaeology. Many state SHPOs require RPA certification. TVAR's team holds RPA credentials and meets all applicable qualification standards.

What is the difference between Phase II evaluation and Phase III data recovery?

Phase II evaluation determines whether a site is significant enough to warrant protection. Phase III data recovery is a mitigation measure undertaken when an eligible site cannot be avoided — it involves extensive excavation to recover and document the site's data before it is impacted by construction or other ground-disturbing activities.

Plan Your Phase II Evaluation with Confidence

Phase II archaeological evaluation is a pivotal step in CRM compliance — and the quality of the work directly affects your project's regulatory outcomes, schedule, and budget. A well-designed evaluation generates defensible eligibility determinations that hold up to SHPO and agency scrutiny, keeping your project moving forward.

Tennessee Valley Archaeological Research brings more than 14 years of experience conducting Phase II evaluations across the Southeast, with RPA-certified archaeologists and in-house GIS capabilities to support every stage of the process.
